# Transcrição: 09-Site Optimisation- Split Testing

- **Fonte:** 09-Site Optimisation- Split Testing
- **Duração:** 17:11
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:06]

Now that we've analyzed our user experience, our landing page, gone deeper into understanding what's working and what's not, the next part of taking all that information is then to run some split tests, some A-B testing on your landing page. It's one of the most simplest and easiest things that you can do, but the majority of people don't take advantage of. So what it is, is the ability to put some code on your site and to take existing content and create a B variation of that page. So for example, if you have a landing page and you want to test whether having a big banner or small banner works better or a blue button or red button, you can create a second variant of that page and play around with the layout and try something different and literally split that traffic between both variants using a tool like Google Optimize. So I use Google Optimize, it's free, it's easy, and for most of what we do at the more basic level, it's good enough. There are many other tools out there as well. It really depends on how much traffic you're pushing through, how complex you want your testing and kind of what that looks like for your business as well. So Google Optimize is a starter optimization platform. It's something which also attaches to Google Analytics natively as well. So that when you're analyzing your performance from your testing, you can see it in the tool itself, so Google Optimize, but it also pushes into the raw data you get from Google Analytics analytics as well. And then you can split your variants. So if you've got enough traffic, you could see mobile versus desktop and things like that. But what I want to show you here is just a real simple test that we ran on one of our landing pages over a period of about a month. And the goal for this test is really, really, really such a simple test. Testing the wording on a landing page for a webinar of saying webinar versus online training. So one of the theories that we had was when people see the word webinar in an ad on a landing page, they kind of, there's an expectation of what a webinar is going to kind of give you and what's going to happen at the end of it. So obviously a lot of the kind of webinars that are advertised are going to offer you something at the end. And a lot of people know this, some people don't. But one of our theories was based on analysis of this particular landing page. So it was a 997 cost webinar. What we wanted to do was test whether changing the word from webinar to online training on the landing page would make any difference at all to people actually signing up. And here's the data that we found. Now we didn't have enough statistical evidence to say that this would work.

## [03:06 - 06:10]

So what I mean by that is we had the original page and a variant which just simply changed out the messaging. And you can see the traffic split here. So 768 visits versus 994. Then you can see the conversion rate from 4.56%. We increased it to 6.34%. That's a huge increase just by changing one word. However, there wasn't enough data to make it highly probable that it would stick. And what I mean by that is when you're running an experiment, you need a statistical significance over 95%. So this test finished with only 84%. Which means it's got a high chance of working. But it's not as guaranteed as having, you know, if we'd run this longer, more traffic and got a higher split, that would have supported our case. And you can kind of see what's happening here with the conversion data over time as well. So the conversion rate for the control, which is our existing page, is the dotted line. And the variant is the blue line. And what you're looking for is for that line to even out over time and stay consistent. So this test was paused earlier than it should have been. So it's just something to be aware of when you are running your tests, not to be caught up in this number, which is the conversion rate increase, but the statistical relevance. Now, in this particular case, the webinar itself, we had to pause it because we were changing the whole webinar. But the data here was super insightful, which kind of went a long way to prove that not only should we consider using online training on our landing pages, but also in our ads, in our emails, and shifting away from, you know, registered for this free webinar to sign up to this online training. So it's a whole, it's a different take on the same thing, but it changes the expectation of people coming in. The second part of this test would then be to see whether people that signed up for the webinar or the online training actually converted to a purchase better. So this is literally just people signing up. It doesn't mean that this variant drove more revenue. And that's the other thing that you need to be aware of is when you're doing these incremental tests, and especially with something like this, where the final goal is quite high up. So it's people to sign up for the registry for the webinar is to then track whether that actually created a monetary benefit for you. So for example, if you're in a product page for e-com, and you've increased the conversion rate from cart page to checkout, if that doesn't come through with higher sales, conversion rate revenue all the way through, then that then there's something else missing. I've seen this before as well. So one of the things about this is just making sure that you're setting up your tests and you're understanding what you're actually influencing and what the data is telling you. So this is just one example of a split test tool. This is my preference

## [06:10 - 09:14]

right now just because it's so easy and fast to get started. And it's something which I recommend everyone do. But what at least my approach is don't just jump into testing and shifting things around your page based on your opinion, do it on data. So everything about the BPM method program is about systems, it's about data, it's about analysis, it's about planning and action. And the same thing applies here that if you are looking to improve the conversion rate on your landing page through your funnel, take a data based view of it. So look at what you can improve and what you can edit. So that's the kind of view, high level view on the kind of data that you can create. There's many different ways of tests that you can analyze as well. So this is a simple A B split test, I could create more variants and have a A B C D test, I could also do a multivariant test. A multivariant test takes an existing page and tests multiple elements on that same page. So in this one, we've created two different pages and split tests, just the headline. But with a multivariant test, you could take that original page, create three or four different headlines, create three or four different banners, etc. Pretty much how a dynamic content optimize ad works in Facebook. So a DCO ad is actually a multivariant tested ad. So multivariant meaning that there's multiple inputs into that ad and then you test it. Same thing applies on your landing page. So that's a multivariant test. Whereas Facebook split test tool is more like the A B test that I've created here, which is you get two different variants, two different versions, and you run that test. So that's just to give you an idea of an example test, how to kind of look at the data and kind of what you're looking for in terms of outcomes as well. Then here, I've got an example to show you how the kind of standard tools work. So this is Google optimize. And the other tools work in fairly similar ways in terms of a kind of WYSIWYG format, what you see is what you get. So you can kind of click elements, you can edit elements, you can move things around, for example. So just like any kind of landing page builder software, a lot of the kind of optimization tools work in in a similar way. So you can select different elements, you can go into the code. So, you know, if you want to change the actual code of a block, for example, and you know, you want to do something a bit more complex, then you can do that. So you can go into edit the HTML, if you're, you know, deep into the coding side of things, and you can do that. Or you can kind of just change elements at face value as well. So if I click on edit text, I could say the only ultimate, etc. I mean, that's just to give you a simple example of how to use it. Now, what you want to be doing is, you know, as I said, you want to plan your testing out. So you know, you've done your analysis, you've done your insights work, you've got an idea of what you want to test.

## [09:14 - 12:19]

And you know, in the previous example, I gave you of just changing one word, webinar versus online training. That was the test, like the hypothesis was, by changing the word from webinar to online testing, we could increase the number of signups. And generally, we saw that. So you know, on this page, I might be looking through looking at minor analysis. And I could be looking at things like bounce rates, where people are clicking. So I would be going through my heat maps and seeing what people are actually doing on this page. And maybe what I need to shift around. So, you know, maybe this one, for example, is something I don't like. And then I kind of click into here. And I click remove. So I just don't want that. Maybe here, for example, we want to, let's click on edit. So here we want to edit the text. Let's change that one. Here we go. So maybe we want to change the font size. And we want to go larger. That's not giving us more options. So let's try 60, for example. And now that font size has become bigger, and it's a bit easier to read, etc, etc. So what I'm doing here in particular is I'm creating a B variant of my page. But going down the page, and for example, ticking off my list of things that I want to test, the thing about running an A and B test is, when I have my original page, which is the page that I am challenging, if I'm doing a split test, I want that B change the challenger page to take into account as many of the elements that I think can help improve it. You could, for example, do something simple as changing just one line of copy like we did on the previous test, or you could go through and change many elements at the same time. If you take the second route, part of the challenge is knowing what's going to work or what actually is working in that as well. So there's two sides of that. One is if you run it as a multivariant test, then you can test multiple options in that particular page. So, you know, to give you a simple example, through the program, we've talked about dynamic content optimized ads within Facebook, they are multivariate ads. What that means is you can have three versions of the copy, three versions of the headline, three versions of the image or the video or whatever it is, you create multiple options, and every kind of option multiplies the previous. So if I've got three headlines, three copy blocks and three images, that's three times three times three variants, that's 27 variants just within that one ad. And the same thing can apply to your landing page. So for example, if I ran a multivariant test here, I could test the word the ultimate, the only ultimate, the amazing ultimate, that's three variants already. And if I start adding other variants to the page,

## [12:19 - 15:22]

then that the tool will create those as different variants. So variant one would have option one copy with, you know, this image, variant two would have option one copy with a different image. So you can get quite complex in your testing, that will give you more precise information as to which of those blends are working. The analogy I use with this is, if you are in a restaurant, let's say you've got a restaurant and you sell pizzas, and you want to know which toppings people like. Now, you might have a simple margarita, which is tomato and cheese, then you might have a margarita that just has pineapple on it, margarita that just has mushroom on it, etc, etc. To test all those variants in a single format is going to take quite a lot of pizzas and a lot of people to come in. Or what you could do is say, I'm going to test margarita versus this vegetable option, which has mushroom, olives, sweet corn, etc. And then a meat version, which has pepperoni and chicken, for example. So you've only got three pizzas there. You want to the first test would be which of those three is selling the best. Then within that, let's say, for example, the meat and the vegetarian do really well, then you might run different versions of that with different toppings and kind of things like that. That's the way I see my testing is in the first instance, I'm going to test some very different options. So for example, this landing page, if I was to create a B variant of this, I would change things around quite fundamentally to give my give me a chance of seeing how different the experience could be. And whether it makes any kind of difference at all. So based on data, based on analysis, but looking on kind of click data and what people are doing on this page, I might create some variants to see what we can do to really shift conversion. So, you know, the first example is just shifting one thing at a time. But that one thing might not be big enough, it may may take too long to test. Whereas if I'm doing multivariate testing and testing multiple things at the same time, I can now start to increase the options and availability of my feedback on the testing. But that requires traffic. So if you're just starting out, and you're not getting much traffic, don't change more than one or two elements at a time. If you're now generating large traffic volumes, and you're able to, you know, get more visitors onto your landing page, then it then you can start to introduce more variants and you can create more testing from that as well. And so like I'm looking at if you're getting 100 to 500 visitors a day, keep your variables small, if you're getting 1000 to 10,000 visitors a day, and I'm talking about the landing page level, then you can increase the number of options and testing that you can create. Because what you want to do is to create the best outcomes. So going back to this example, for example, over a period of a month, the traffic here was tiny. But because we only had one variant, it wasn't so bad. Even then, we'd still want more traffic over a month to kind of get enough data,

## [15:22 - 17:11]

or continue running the test for a longer period of time, which, you know, if you're just making one change, you want to kind of get that outcome and the feedback quite quickly as well. So there are considerations to have in place when you're setting up your testing, but it all starts with data, getting your hypotheses and ideas in place. Often what I do is take my page design, print it on a piece of paper, physically go through it and mark out what I want to do to change it. Or for example, I might record a loom video or something where I can feed it back to a designer or developer and say, like, I like this, don't want to change this, I want to move this image over here. And that gives that person an idea of what to come up with with your B variant. And, you know, you can work with a designer, you can push all this to a designer, and then get your tech person or developer to code that into a B version of the page. And then you can run that through your split test tool. You don't have to create your variant in this tool either. If, for example, you're using a landing page builder, many of them come with split test options, if they don't, you can create your B and C variant in your landing page tool, plug them into something like Google Optimize. And within that, all you do is say, variant, my variant A is, you know, this new page, my variant B is this new page. And all the tool will do is split the traffic between those pages. So this is giving you an example of going into the actual builder and moving things around. If you have separate pages, and you want to test that, you can also do that through your Optimize tool. You can also do that through Facebook split test as well. So it's just really thinking about where you want that testing to focus in on, and how you want to run that test in order to decide how to set it up. And it all comes through planning and building the process.
